step_outputs.6:
Three Blood Drops

The thing about fake blood is that everyone thinks they know how to make it, but nobody actually does. Take the year eights trying to sneak into Frightmare Manor tonight - their "blood" was literally just food colouring and water. Amateurs. You need corn syrup for viscosity, chocolate powder for depth of colour, and just a hint of coffee to get that metallic undertone. Plus about six other secret ingredients that I'm not sharing because I didn't spend three years perfecting my recipe just to give it away.

"Penny! There's a situation in the Zombie Zone!" Marcus's voice crackled through my headset. Tuesday nights are usually dead (ha), but half term means dealing with groups of kids trying to prove something.

I found them by following the high-pitched screaming. Three year eights, still in their school uniforms, had managed to get past security and were now trapped between Zombie Zone and Vampire Villa, clutching their pathetic bottles of red water.

"Evening," I said, clicking on my torch. "Having fun?"

They jumped satisfyingly. The tallest one tried to look tough but had smeared his food colouring across his cheek.

"We paid!" squeaked the shortest one, who was definitely lying.

"Really? Because I do the staff scheduling and there's no school groups booked tonight." I pulled out my phone and pretended to check. "Unless you're the St. Michael's Drama Club? The ones coming tomorrow?"

"Yeah! That's us!"

"Interesting. Because that booking's for Thursday."

The middle one elbowed the liar. I sighed and pointed my torch toward the exit. "Right. Out. Before I call your school. And by the way?" I gestured to their bottles. "If you're trying to make fake blood, you need to add corn syrup. Food colouring just makes you look desperate."

They shuffled out, but not before I heard one mutter, "Know-it-all." I wanted to tell them that actually, I'm just good at my job, but explaining yourself to year eights is a lost cause.

Back in the effects room, I checked my phone while cleaning prosthetics. Mira had sent sixteen messages to our group chat about drama school applications.

Mira: THEY ADDED ANOTHER AUDITION REQUIREMENT
Mira: Now we need to do a "contemporary monologue showing emotional range"
Mira: What does that even mean???
Mira: Penny where are you
Mira: This is a crisis
Mira: An EMOTIONAL RANGE crisis

I started typing a response about how she should do that bit from Fleabag, then deleted it. Mum had seen me researching drama schools last week and given me another lecture about "sensible careers" and "living in the real world." As if special effects isn't a real job. As if I haven't been practically running Frightmare's effects department since summer.

My phone buzzed again. Unknown number.

Three blood drop emojis. Just that.

"Very scary," I muttered, but checked the locks on the effects room doors anyway. Probably just someone who'd seen me telling off the year eights. Or another drama student trying to prove something about practical effects versus digital.

I turned back to the prosthetic zombie hand I was cleaning. The fake blood I'd used tonight was some of my best work - perfect viscosity, just the right amount of clotting around the edges. I was trying to figure out how to mention it in my drama school personal statement without sounding weird when the lights went out.

Not just the effects room. The whole building.

My phone lit up again. Same number.

🩸🩸🩸

Right. Okay. Power cuts happen. And repeated creepy texts are just... a thing that happens. To be expected when you work in a haunted house. Even if we've never actually had a power cut before. Even if the emergency lights should have kicked in by now.

I used my phone torch to find the door, trying to remember if I'd told Jamie where I hide the spare effects key. He's been weirdly interested in my fake blood recipes lately. If this is his idea of a prank...

The torch beam caught something on the floor. A drop of red liquid.

Then another.

Then another.

I knelt down and touched one carefully. Still wet. Definitely not mine - wrong consistency, too thin. More like...

The lights slammed back on and I jumped, heart racing. The drops were gone. Just the clean floor, the organized shelves of supplies, everything exactly where it should be.

My phone buzzed one more time.

Mira: PENNY SERIOUSLY WHERE ARE YOU
Mira: I'm having an emotional range emergency!!!

I looked at the floor again. Nothing there. Obviously nothing there. Because I'm not the kind of person who sees things in the dark. I'm the kind of person who makes other people see things in the dark. That's literally my job.

I texted Mira back: On my way home. Crisis management in 10.

Then I packed up quickly, definitely not running out of the building, definitely not imagining more red drops appearing behind me. Because that would be ridiculous. I deal in fake scares, not real ones.

But I saved that unknown number, just in case.

And if I checked my effects supplies three times before leaving, well, that's just being professional. Nothing to do with the way my hands were shaking, or how the shadows seemed deeper than usual, or how every splash of my award-worthy fake blood suddenly looked a bit too real.

Tomorrow I'd figure out which year nine thought this was funny. Tomorrow I'd be properly angry about it. Tomorrow I'd tell Mira and we'd laugh about how horror movies have ruined text messages forever.

But tonight?

Tonight I ran home through the rain, phone clutched tight, trying not to think about the fact that those three drops had looked exactly like my recipe. The one I've never shared with anyone.

The one that's supposed to be impossible to copy.
step_outputs.7:
The Group Chat Explodes

Everyone at sixth form was talking about Ellie Chen disappearing, which made my own creepy text drama feel a bit self-indulgent. Ellie was in my GCSE Drama group last year - not exactly friends, but she'd borrowed my liquid latex once and returned it mostly full.

"She's probably just at her dad's," Sophie Miller was telling everyone in the common room. "Her parents are divorced and-"

"Her dad lives in Hong Kong," Mira interrupted, not looking up from her phone. "And she missed her university interview yesterday. Ellie Chen would never miss a university interview."

I was trying to focus on my drama school application while pretending to study Business Economics. My personal statement still sounded fake. How do you explain that you're genuinely passionate about making people believe in monsters?

My phone buzzed. I jumped, then tried to pretend I was just stretching.

Mira noticed anyway. "What's wrong with you? You've been weird since last night."

"Nothing's wrong. I'm fine. Everything's fine."

"You've said 'fine' three times."

"Because it is fine."

"Four times."

I showed her the text from last night. She gasped so loudly that Sophie looked over.

"What?"

"Nothing," we said together.

Mira waited until Sophie went back to her Ellie Chen theories before whispering, "Blood emojis? At midnight? While you were alone in a haunted house? This is literally how every true crime podcast starts!"

"It's not-"

"And now Ellie's missing?"

"Those things aren't connected."

"Five years of friendship and you still don't trust my true crime expertise?"

"I trust that you've listened to too many murder podcasts."

"There's no such thing as too many murder podcasts."

My phone buzzed again. Unknown number.

🩸🩸🩸

Mira grabbed my phone. "It's happening again! In daylight! This is definitely serial killer behavior."

"It's definitely someone being a prat," I said, taking my phone back. "Probably the same year eights from last night."

"What year eights?"

I told her about the fake blood idiots. She started making notes in her phone.

"What are you doing?"

"Documenting potential suspects. This could be revenge for you embarrassing them."

"They're thirteen. They can barely manage food colouring."

"Ted Bundy started young."

"Ted Bundy didn't use emoji."

The bell rang. As we headed to class, my phone kept buzzing with group chat notifications:

Sophie: police came to my house about ellie
Sophie: apparently her phone last pinged near frightmare manor!!
Leo: weird, penny was working there last night
Leo: you see anything?
Mira: 👀
Me: Just some year 8s trying to sneak in
Me: Nothing weird
Mira: NOTHING WEIRD?? YOU GOT THE BLOOD TEXTS
Sophie: what blood texts??
Me: MIRA
Mira: sorry it slipped out
Leo: what blood texts??
Me: It's nothing
Sophie: PENNY
Me: Just someone being stupid
Leo: at frightmare?
Leo: where ellie's phone was??
Me: It's not connected
Mira: everything's connected
Me: Stop helping

I silenced my phone before History, but couldn't focus. Mr. Patterson was talking about the Cold War but all I could think about was those blood drops on the effects room floor. The ones that definitely hadn't been real. Definitely.

After class, I headed straight to Frightmare. Tuesday afternoons are prep time - checking supplies, mixing blood, fixing prosthetics. My favourite part of the job, usually.

The effects room looked normal. Everything where I'd left it. But something felt...off.

My special effects logbook was slightly askew on the desk. I always leave it perfectly aligned with the edge. Always.

I opened it carefully. There, between my notes on blood consistency and latex application, was a new entry:

NICE RECIPE. NEEDS MORE IRON THOUGH.

The handwriting was weird - too neat, like someone trying to disguise their normal style.

My phone buzzed again. I nearly dropped it.

But it was just Jamie: can I borrow some fake blood?

Me: No
Jamie: please
Jamie: it's for a project
Me: What project?
Jamie: a secret project
Me: No
Jamie: I'll tell mum about the drama school applications
Me: You wouldn't
Jamie: try me

I was composing a detailed threat about what would happen to his gaming setup if he dared when I noticed something else wrong. My main effects kit was slightly unzipped. Just a centimeter, but I never leave it unzipped. Never.

Inside, everything looked normal at first. Then I noticed the blood bottle. The lid was slightly unscrewed.

I opened it carefully. Sniffed. Something was different. The consistency was wrong. More metallic.

More like...

No. No way. That's ridiculous.

My phone buzzed one more time. Unknown number again.

But not emojis this time.

GREAT RECIPE. MADE SOME IMPROVEMENTS. HOPE YOU DON'T MIND.

I dropped the bottle. It hit the floor but didn't break. Just leaked slowly, making a pattern that looked exactly like the drops from last night.

Exactly like them.

Because they were the same drops.

Because someone had been in here before me.

Someone who knew my recipe.

Someone who'd made it better.

My phone buzzed again. Group chat.

Sophie: guys
Sophie: they found ellie's phone
Sophie: in the frightmare manor car park
Sophie: covered in blood
step_outputs.8:
Fake Blood, Real Problems

"There has to be a logical explanation," I said for the eighteenth time, pacing Mira's bedroom while she added red string to her evidence wall. Yes, she actually had an evidence wall. She'd started it an hour ago.

"The logical explanation is that someone's recreating horror movies using your fake blood recipes," she said, pinning up a photo of Ellie from Instagram. "And you have to admit, your blood is the most realistic in Cheltenham."

"Thanks, but-"

"The question is why? Revenge? Art? Statement about society?"

"Or someone's just trying to scare me."

"While a girl is missing? That's one hell of a coincidence."

My phone buzzed. Just Jamie: where did you hide the corn syrup?

Me: I told you no
Jamie: found it nvm

I should probably worry about that, but there were more pressing issues. Like the fact that someone had broken into my effects room. Or that Ellie's phone was apparently covered in something that looked exactly like my recipe. Or that my Business Economics coursework was due tomorrow and I hadn't started it because I'd been working on drama school applications instead.

Mira was still talking. "We need to investigate properly. I've made a list of suspects."

She handed me her phone. The notes app was terrifyingly organized:

SUSPECTS:
1. Year 8s - revenge motive but limited skills
2. Other drama students - jealous of effects expertise?
3. Leo from tech - suspicious knowledge of blood recipes
4. New security guard - access to building
5. Jamie?????? (suspicious behavior lately)

"My brother is not a suspect."

"He's been asking about your recipes."

"He's thirteen!"

"Same age as the year 8s you ruled out for being too young."

Fair point. But before I could argue, my phone rang. Mum.

"Where are you?"

"At Mira's. Working on coursework."

"Well, come home. Family dinner tonight, remember?"

I had not remembered. Because Tuesday is never family dinner night. Tuesday is effects prep night. Everyone knows this.

"But I have to work-"

"Penny. Home. Now."

She hung up before I could argue. Mira looked sympathetic. "Family dinner?"

"Apparently."

"Want me to check the effects room while you're gone?"

"No investigating without me."

"But-"

"Mira."

"Fine. But text me if anything weird happens."

The walk home was tense. Every shadow looked wrong. Every puddle looked too red. Every notification made me jump.

Jamie was waiting at the front door. "Finally! Did you see what I did with the corn syrup?"

"What did you do with the corn syrup?"

He grinned and pulled me into his room. On his desk was... actually a pretty decent zombie bite wound. The blood consistency was almost perfect.

"How did you-"

"I watched your tutorial videos."

"What tutorial videos?"

"The ones you made last year? On your old phone? The password was still 'Hamlet2019'."

I was going to kill him. After I changed all my passwords.

"Jamie! Penny! Dinner!" Mum called.

At the table, Dad was already talking about university prospectuses. "There's a brilliant Business program at Bath-"

"I told you, I'm taking a gap year," I said automatically, then regretted it when everyone got quiet.

"To do what?" Mum asked carefully.

Work on special effects. Apply to drama school. Follow my actual dreams instead of their sensible ones.

"Travel," I said instead. "Maybe."

Jamie snorted into his pasta. I kicked him under the table.

My phone buzzed. Unknown number.

DINNER WITH THE FAMILY? SWEET. SHAME ABOUT ELLIE MISSING HERS.

I dropped my fork.

"Penny?" Mum asked.

"Fine! Everything's fine."

Jamie was watching me. He knew something. He definitely knew something.

After dinner, I cornered him in the hallway. "What do you know?"

"About what?"

"Don't play dumb. You've been weird all week."

"You're the one being weird."

"Jamie."

He shifted uncomfortably. "I might have seen something. At Frightmare. Last week."

"What?"

"Can't say."

"Jamie!"

"Not here," he whispered. "Later. After-"

Mum appeared with dessert. "Who wants ice cream?"

By the time I got back to my room, I had seventeen messages from Mira:

EMERGENCY
Someone broke into the effects room again
But they didn't take anything
They left something
You need to see this
Penny seriously
Answer your phone
This is proper creepy
Like proper proper creepy
I called the police
They're not taking it seriously
But you really need to see this
Like right now
Don't freak out
But also maybe freak out a little
Are you seeing these messages??
PENNY

I was already climbing out my window before I finished reading. The last time Mira called something "proper proper creepy" was when we found that abandoned theatre in year eight. And that had actually been proper proper creepy.

My phone buzzed one more time. Unknown number.

LEAVING WITHOUT TELLING YOUR PARENTS? TISK TISK. ELLIE DID THAT TOO.

I nearly fell off the drainpipe.

Below me, Jamie's window lit up. I could see him at his desk, working on something red and sticky.

Practicing.

Or preparing.
step_outputs.9:
Family Matters

Parents' evening is awkward enough without having to investigate mysterious blood texts between appointments. Mira and I were huddled in the corner of the sixth form hall while Mum and Dad talked to Mr. Patterson about my "concerning lack of focus in History."

"Show me again what you found," I whispered.

Mira pulled up the photo on her phone. The effects room had looked normal at first glance, except for my logbook being open to a new page. Someone had used my fake blood to write:

GOOD ARTISTS COPY
GREAT ARTISTS STEAL
BEST ARTISTS IMPROVE

"And you're sure it was fresh?"

"Still wet when I found it. And look at the consistency - exactly like your recipe."

She was right. The drips had that perfect viscosity I'd spent years perfecting. Which meant either someone had stolen my recipe book, or...

"Jamie," I muttered.

"Your brother? What about him?"

Before I could answer, Jamie himself appeared, looking suspiciously smart in his actual school uniform instead of his usual hoodie.

"Mum says stop whispering and come to Business Studies. They're waiting."

"I need to talk to you first."

"Can't. Got my own appointments." He started walking away, then turned back. "Oh, and thanks for the corn syrup recipe. Really helped with the consistency."

He disappeared into the crowd before I could grab him.

Mira raised an eyebrow. "Okay, that was properly suspicious."

"He's been practicing effects makeup. Found my old tutorial videos."

"The ones where you explained your blood recipe in detail?"

"...yeah."

"The recipe someone's now using to leave creepy messages?"

"It's not Jamie. He's weird but he's not that weird."

My phone buzzed. Unknown number.

FAILING BUSINESS STUDIES? TSK TSK. MAYBE YOU SHOULD FOCUS LESS ON FAKE BLOOD AND MORE ON REAL LIFE.

"How do they know about Business Studies?" Mira whispered.

I looked around the crowded hall. Dozens of students, parents, teachers. Anyone could be watching.

"Penny!" Mum called. "Now, please!"

Mrs. Wright, my Business teacher, was already talking as I sat down. "...concerning pattern of distraction. Last week's assignment on profit margins was particularly disappointing."

Because I'd been working on my drama school application instead. Not that I could say that.

"I'll do better," I muttered.

"It's not about doing better, it's about applying yourself. Your project on special effects marketing showed real promise-"

My phone buzzed again. I tried to ignore it.

And again.

And again.

Mum shot me a look. I checked under the desk.

MARKETING PROJECT WAS GOOD
VERY CREATIVE
SHAME ABOUT THE BLOOD STAINS THOUGH

What blood stains?

I pulled my project folder from my bag. There, on the last page, was a perfect red fingerprint.

Fresh.

My phone buzzed one more time:

OOPS. MY BAD.

I stood up so quickly my chair fell over.

"Penny?" Mrs. Wright asked.

"Bathroom," I managed, already running.

In the hallway, I called Jamie. No answer. Texted him:

WHERE ARE YOU?
This isn't funny
Jamie seriously
Answer me

Mira found me by the lockers. "What happened?"

I showed her the fingerprint.

"That's... that's not your fake blood, is it?"

"Wrong consistency. Too thin. More like..."

"Real blood?"

We stared at each other.

"We have to tell someone," she said.

"Tell them what? That someone's leaving creepy messages in fake blood that might be real blood and my brother might be involved but also might not be and also Ellie Chen is still missing and-"

"Penny?"

We jumped. Jamie was standing there, looking weirdly nervous.

"Where have you been?" I demanded.

"Had to talk to someone."

"Who?"

"Can't say."

"Jamie, if you know something about Ellie-"

"Not here," he whispered. "Too many people. But... I saw something last week. At Frightmare. Something proper weird."

"What kind of weird?"

He glanced around the crowded hallway. "Meet me tonight? At the manor? After closing?"

"Why not just tell me now?"

"Because..." He stopped. Looked over my shoulder. Went pale.

I turned. Nothing there. When I looked back, he was gone.

My phone buzzed.

LITTLE BROTHERS CAN BE SO ANNOYING
WANT ME TO DEAL WITH HIM?

"No no no," I muttered, calling Jamie again. Straight to voicemail.

Mira was already adding to her notes. "So Jamie definitely knows something."

"We have to find him."

"Your parents are still in with Mrs. Wright."

"I don't care. Something's wrong. He's never this weird."

"He's thirteen. He's always weird."

"Not this weird. Not scared weird."

My phone buzzed again. Unknown number.

But this time, just a location pin. For Frightmare Manor.

And a time: Midnight.

And three blood drops.

"Don't even think about it," Mira said.

"He's my brother."

"It's obviously a trap."

"Obviously."

"So we're not going?"

I looked at her.

She sighed. "Fine. But I'm bringing my true crime emergency kit."

"Your what?"

"Don't ask questions you don't want answers to."

My phone buzzed one last time:

FAMILY MEETING AT MIDNIGHT
DON'T BE LATE
JAMIE'S DYING TO SHOW YOU HIS NEW EFFECTS
LITERALLY
step_outputs.10:
Opening Night

Halloween week at Frightmare Manor is usually my favorite time of year. Tonight, watching fake zombies stumble past fake vampires while fake blood drips aesthetically down fake walls, all I could think about was how many places someone could hide a real body.

"Stop catastrophizing," Mira whispered through my headset. She'd convinced Marcus she was my new effects assistant. "Jamie's fine. He's probably just being a dramatic teenager."

"He's not answering his phone."

"Because he's at football practice. I checked his schedule."

"How do you have his schedule?"

"I have everyone's schedule. For investigative purposes."

I should probably be worried about that, but right now I was more worried about the crowd forming outside the effects room. Halloween week means endless complaints about makeup and prosthetics.

"The blood keeps dripping in my eyes," zombie number three was saying.

"That's because you keep touching it," I replied automatically, while scanning the queue for Jamie. He had to be here somewhere. "Just leave it alone and-"

My phone buzzed.

SUCH A GOOD SISTER
ALWAYS HELPING OTHERS
SHAME YOU COULDN'T HELP ELLIE
OR JAMIE

"Ignore it," Mira said in my ear. "They're just trying to get to you."

Another buzz.

WANT TO KNOW WHAT YOUR BROTHER SAW?
MEET ME IN THE BASEMENT
ALONE

"Don't even think about it," Mira warned.

"I wasn't."

"You were. I can hear you thinking about it."

She was right. But before I could reply, I spotted something in the zombie queue. A familiar hoodie. Jamie's hoodie.

But not Jamie wearing it.

I pushed through the zombies, ignoring their complaints about smudged makeup. The person in Jamie's hoodie disappeared around a corner.

"What are you doing?" Mira asked.

"Following a lead."

"What lead?"

"Jamie's hoodie."

"On who?"

"Don't know yet."

The figure was always just ahead, just around the next corner, just out of reach. Leading me deeper into the manor, past the public areas, towards the old basement stairs.

My phone buzzed again.

GETTING WARMER

"Penny, wait," Mira said. "This is literally how every horror movie starts."

"Got a better idea?"

"Several. All involving not following mysterious figures into basements."

But I was already heading down the stairs. The figure had disappeared, but Jamie's hoodie was draped over the basement door handle.

My phone lit up.

GETTING VERY WARM

"Mira?"

"Still here. Still thinking this is a terrible idea."

"If I'm not back in five minutes..."

"Already called backup."

"What backup?"

"Don't ask questions you don't want answered."

The basement door creaked open. Because of course it did. Everything about this was becoming horrifically cliché.

My torch beam caught something red on the floor. A trail of drops leading deeper into the basement.

Real blood this time. I was sure of it. The consistency was wrong for my recipe. Too thin. Too metallic.

My phone buzzed one last time.

BOILING HOT

"Penny?" Mira's voice was breaking up. "Signal's... losing you..."

The headset crackled and went dead.

Of course it did.

The blood trail led to the old props storage room. The door was slightly open. A light was on inside.

I should wait for Mira's mysterious backup.

I should call the police.

I should do anything except what I was about to do.

I pushed the door open.

The room was full of old set pieces and broken props. And there, in the center, was Jamie's phone. Playing a video.

Of Jamie.

In the effects room.

Last Tuesday.

He was hiding behind a shelf, filming something. The camera shook as he whispered, "I knew it. I knew something weird was happening. Look at-"

The video cut off.

My phone buzzed.

WANT TO SEE HOW IT ENDS?

Another buzz.

CHECK THE SUPPLY CUPBOARD

The supply cupboard door was closed. A red handprint marked the handle.

I reached for it.

Stopped.

Looked closer at the handprint.

Something wasn't right.

The consistency was...

"Oh," I said out loud. "Oh, you absolute-"

I touched the handprint. Still wet.

But not blood.

My recipe. My exact recipe. Down to the coffee undertones and chocolate powder base.

Which meant...

"Looking for this?"

I turned.

And there was Jamie, very much alive, holding an empty bottle of my fake blood.

And grinning.

"Got you," he said.

Then the lights went out.

And my phone buzzed one final time.

FOUND YOU

But not from the unknown number this time.

From Jamie's phone.

The one that was supposedly on the floor in front of me.

The one that was now lighting up someone else's face in the darkness.

Someone holding something that looked a lot like my special effects knife.

Except the blade was real.

And so was the blood on it.
step_outputs.11:
Stage Fright

"Run," Jamie whispered.

I grabbed his arm and pulled him behind a stack of old set pieces as the knife-wielding figure stepped into the room. In the dim light from their phone screen, I could see they were wearing one of our zombie masks. One of my zombie masks. The good ones, with the detailed decomposition around the jaw.

Professionally insulting, really.

Jamie was trembling. I squeezed his arm, hoping it felt reassuring rather than terrified.

"When I said I needed to show you something," he whispered, "this wasn't it."

"What was it?"

"Last week I saw someone stealing your recipe book. I tried to film them but-"

A prop crashed nearby. We both jumped.

"Come out, come out," sang a voice. Familiar, but distorted by the mask. "Don't you want to know what happened to Ellie?"

Jamie's hand tightened on my arm. "Penny, I think they-"

"Shh."

More props falling. Closer now.

My phone buzzed. I silenced it without looking, but not before the light gave away our position.

"There you are!"

We ran.

Through the basement, up the stairs, past surprised zombies and vampires who probably thought this was part of the show. My headset was still dead. No way to call for help.

"The effects room," Jamie panted. "We can lock ourselves in."

"They'll have the key."

"But all your weapons are fake."

"They don't know that."

We sprinted down the corridor, dodging confused customers. Behind us, the masked figure walked calmly, unhurried. Like they knew something we didn't.

The effects room door was open.

It shouldn't have been open.

"Wait-" I started, but Jamie was already running in.

The door slammed behind him.

Leaving me in the corridor.

With the masked figure at one end.

And another figure, wearing my spare zombie mask, at the other.

My phone buzzed.

SURPRISE

"Penny!" Jamie's voice from inside the effects room. "They've blocked the door!"

I looked between the two masked figures. Both holding real knives. Both moving closer.

Think, think, think.

What would a horror movie character do?

Die, probably.

What would a special effects expert do?

I reached into my pocket and pulled out my emergency fake blood capsules. The ones that burst on impact.

And threw them at the nearest light fixture.

Red liquid exploded across the bulb.

The corridor went dark.

Someone swore.

I dropped to the ground as the figures crashed into each other in the darkness.

Crawled to the effects room door.

"Jamie! The window!"

"It's stuck!"

"Use the fake arm!"

"What?"

"The prosthetic arm! In the box by the desk! Use it to break the window!"

A crash from inside.

Then another crash behind me as one of the masked figures found the light switch.

The corridor blazed back into view.

Both figures were still standing.

Both still armed.

Both looking at me.

My phone buzzed one last time.

GAME OVER

But they'd forgotten something.

This was Halloween week.

Which meant...

"NOW!" I screamed.

Right on cue, the manor's automated scream track started playing at full volume.

The figures flinched.

Just for a second.

But long enough for Jamie to reach through the broken window and unlock the effects room door from the outside.

Long enough for me to dive through.

Long enough to slam it shut behind me and turn the lock.

"What now?" Jamie asked, bleeding slightly from where he'd broken the window.

I looked around my effects room. At all my carefully organized supplies. My perfectly crafted fake weapons.

And smiled.

"Now," I said, "we show them what practical effects can really do."

I grabbed my recipe book and turned to the section marked 'Emergency Contingencies.'

"Jamie, how do you feel about being a stunt double?"

He grinned. "Will it involve explosions?"

"If we do it right."

Behind us, someone started banging on the door.

"They'll break through eventually," Jamie said.

"Good."

"Good?"

I pulled out my special effects kit. The industrial one. The one with the theatrical smoke capsules and quick-change prosthetics.

"Let them come," I said. "This is my stage."

My phone buzzed one last time.

FINAL ACT BEGINNING
BREAK A LEG
LITERALLY

"Ready?" I asked Jamie.

He picked up a bottle of fake blood. "Ready."

The door began to splinter.

Time for the performance of my life.

Literally.
step_outputs.12:
Technical Difficulties

The thing about practical effects is that they only have to work once. And they have to work perfectly. No second takes in live performance.

As the door splintered, I ran through the plan one last time.

"Smoke capsules?" I whispered.

Jamie held them up. "Check."

"Quick-change prosthetics?"

"Check."

"Emergency blood packs?"

"Triple check."

"Mira's backup?"

"What backup?"

The door crashed open.

Showtime.

The first masked figure stepped through the smoke capsules we'd thrown, knife raised. The second followed, moving more cautiously.

"That's a terrible mask," I said to the first one. "The latex work is sloppy."

They lunged.

I ducked.

Jamie triggered the blood pack.

Red sprayed across the room, catching both figures by surprise. While they were distracted, I grabbed my emergency kit and ran for the supply closet.

"After her!" one of them shouted. Still familiar. Still distorted.

I slammed the closet door and started the quick-change. The fastest costume switch I'd ever attempted, and this time it actually mattered.

Outside, I heard Jamie yell, then a crash.

Faster, faster.

More crashes.

Almost done.

"Found you," said a voice right outside the closet.

The door flew open.

But I wasn't there.

Instead, they found themselves face to face with... themselves.

I'd copied their mask exactly. Down to the badly done latex work.

In the confusion, I slipped past them into the effects room.

Where I found Jamie wrestling with the second figure, who was trying to reach something on the high shelf.

My recipe book.

"It was never about Ellie, was it?" I said. "This was about my recipes."

The first figure laughed behind their mask. "Finally figured it out?"

"The blood drops were a bit obvious. Real blood doesn't dry like that."

"Noticed that, did you?"

"I noticed you used my recipe. Badly."

That got them. They stepped forward, knife raised. "Badly?"

"Amateur work. Too much corn syrup."

"You think you're so clever."

"Clever enough to know who you are."

They stopped. "Really?"

"The drama teacher wouldn't get the recipe wrong."

Behind them, Jamie's eyes widened. He got it.

"Security wouldn't care about consistency."

The figure took another step.

"But someone who lost the lead makeup artist job to a teenager?"

They lunged.

I threw my last smoke capsule.

The room filled with theatrical fog.

Someone screamed.

Something crashed.

A knife clattered to the floor.

The smoke began to clear.

And there was Sophie Miller, her zombie mask fallen off, being held down by... Mira?

"Took you long enough," I said.

"Dramatic timing," Mira replied, sitting on Sophie while texting someone. "Also, traffic."

The second figure tried to run.

Right into Leo from tech crew, holding what looked like a real cricket bat.

"That's not a prop," he said helpfully.

Sophie was crying now. "It's not fair! I trained for years and they gave the job to you!"

"So you decided to steal my recipes?"

"And frame you for Ellie's disappearance," Mira added. "Speaking of which..."

The police burst in.

Actually burst in, like in a movie.

"Perfect timing," Mira said. "I told you when to arrive for maximum dramatic effect."

Sophie was still talking. "It was supposed to be simple. Steal the recipes, scare you into quitting, make it look like you were involved with Ellie..."

"Where is Ellie?" one of the police officers asked.

Sophie laughed. "At her aunt's in Bristol. I just paid her to turn off her phone for a few days. Made the blood texts more convincing."

"The blood texts were a bit much," I said.

"Had to make it dramatic. You always said theatre needs drama."

"Speaking of drama," Jamie cut in, "can someone explain why Leo and Mira were ready with a cricket bat?"

"I called them," he said. "After I saw Sophie stealing your recipes. But then she caught me filming..."

"So you decided to play along?"

"Wanted to catch her properly. With evidence."

"And you didn't tell me because..."

"You're a terrible actor."

"I am not!"

"Remember the year three nativity?"

"I was a convincing sheep!"

"You were not."

Sophie started laughing hysterically as the police led her away. "You're all insane. This whole place is insane."

"Says the person who sent blood emoji texts," Mira muttered.

My phone buzzed one last time.

NICE PERFORMANCE
SHAME ABOUT THE ENDING
BUT THERE'S ALWAYS THE SEQUEL

"That's not Sophie's phone," Jamie said.

We all looked at each other.

Then at the second mask on the floor.

The one Leo hadn't caught.

The one that was missing.

Along with my recipe book.

"Oh," I said.

"Oh no," said Mira.

My phone buzzed again.

INTERMISSION OVER
ACT TWO BEGINNING
BREAK A LEG
FOR REAL THIS TIME
step_outputs.13:
Notes from the Director

The thing about being a special effects artist is that you learn to appreciate good theatre. And whoever took my recipe book? That was good theatre.

Two weeks after the Sophie incident, things had mostly returned to normal. Ellie was back from Bristol, extremely apologetic about the whole thing. The police had closed the case. Even my parents had calmed down about finding out I'd been secretly applying to drama schools.

"It shows initiative," Dad had said, surprisingly. "Very entrepreneurial."

"The special effects industry is actually quite profitable," Mum added, less surprisingly.

Jamie was unofficially my assistant now, which mostly meant he stole less of my supplies. Mira had taken down her evidence wall, though I caught her adding new string sometimes when she thought I wasn't looking.

Everything was fine.

Except.

"The consistency is wrong," I said, examining the latest blood splatter at Frightmare. "Too thick."

"Maybe they're still learning," Mira suggested.

"They have my recipe book."

"Maybe they're improving it."

"Nobody improves my recipes."

My phone buzzed.

EVERYONE'S A CRITIC

I didn't jump anymore when these came through. They'd become almost routine. Every few days, a new message. Always about blood. Always about theatre.

Never threatening anymore. More like... notes from a director.

GOOD SCENE TODAY
LIGHTING NEEDS WORK
BLOOD SPLATTER TOO OBVIOUS

"You know who it is, don't you?" Jamie asked one night, while we were cleaning prosthetics.

"I have theories."

"Going to share them?"

"Not yet."

"Why not?"

Because sometimes good theatre needs mystery. Because sometimes the not knowing is better than the knowing. Because sometimes...

My phone buzzed.

READY FOR YOUR NEXT PERFORMANCE?

I smiled. "Because they're not done teaching."

Jamie rolled his eyes. "You're both weird, you know that?"

"Both?"

"You and whoever's sending these. Proper theatre people."

"There's nothing proper about us."

"That's what I mean."

My phone buzzed again.

DRAMA SCHOOL AUDITION TOMORROW
BREAK A LEG
NO BLOOD REQUIRED
THIS TIME

I looked at my perfectly organized effects room. At my carefully labeled bottles of fake blood. At the empty space where my recipe book used to be.

And realized something.

I didn't need it anymore.

I'd memorized those recipes years ago. The book was just for show. Just theatre.

Like the blood emoji texts had been theatre.

Like Sophie's mask had been theatre.

Like everything in this haunted house was theatre.

My phone buzzed one last time.

GOOD LUCK
YOU'LL NAIL IT
YOU ALWAYS DO
- THE DIRECTOR

"Are you ever going to tell me who it is?" Jamie asked.

"Would it make a better story if I did?"

He thought about it. "Probably not."

"Then no."

I picked up a bottle of my latest recipe. The consistency was perfect. Better than the old one. Because whoever took my book was right - there's always room for improvement.

Even in fake blood.

Even in theatre.

Even in life.

My phone buzzed again. But this time it was Mira:

Found new evidence!!!! Come quick!!!

I looked at Jamie. He grinned.

"Ready for the sequel?" he asked.

"Always."

Because that's the other thing about theatre.

The show must go on.

Even if it's just three blood drops at midnight.

Even if it's just texts from an unknown number.

Even if it's just fake blood and real mystery and the space between performance and reality.

The curtain never really falls.

It just waits for the next act to begin.

My phone buzzed one final time.

🩸🩸🩸

I smiled.

Showtime.

chapter_raw_judge_text.2:
[Analysis]

Chapter 2 of this darkly comic British YA fiction demonstrates considerable competence in voice, pacing, and tonal balance. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions.

VOICE AND CHARACTER: Penny's first-person narration is consistently well-rendered. The detail about Ellie borrowing liquid latex and returning it "mostly full" is an excellent character beat - it establishes Penny's relationship to Ellie through the lens of her craft without being heavy-handed. The internal logic of her character (perfectionist about effects kit alignment, the logbook always perfectly aligned) pays off effectively when these details become plot-relevant. Mira's true crime obsession is deployed with good comic timing, and the Ted Bundy/emoji exchange is genuinely funny without feeling forced. Jamie's blackmail via drama school applications is consistent with his profile and adds texture.

DIALOGUE: The group chat sequence is a genuine strength. It captures authentic teen communication patterns - the way information leaks despite attempts to contain it, the overlapping messages, Mira's "sorry it slipped out" - all ring true. The spoken dialogue is also solid, with the "fine/four times" exchange being a well-worn but effectively executed bit of comic business.

PLOT AND MYSTERY: The chapter escalates tension competently. The logbook entry ("NICE RECIPE. NEEDS MORE IRON THOUGH.") is a strong, creepy detail. The blood bottle discovery and the revelation that someone has been in the effects room builds genuine unease. The chapter ending with Ellie's phone found covered in blood is effective as a cliffhanger. However, the mystery elements are fairly conventional - the "someone was here before me" revelation follows a predictable horror/thriller template.

TONAL BALANCE: The darkly comic tone is well-maintained. The humor doesn't undercut the genuine menace, and the menace doesn't overwhelm the comedy. This is the chapter's strongest achievement.

WEAKNESSES: The chapter is somewhat formulaic in its mystery construction. The "everything felt off" beat followed by specific displaced objects is a well-worn thriller convention. The "More metallic. More like... No. No way. That's ridiculous." internal monologue is a bit clichéd - the ellipsis-and-denial structure is overused in YA fiction. The chapter follows its plan faithfully but doesn't significantly exceed it. Some of the group chat feels slightly convenient as an exposition delivery mechanism. The chapter doesn't quite achieve the "unresolved threads and messy emotions" the plan promised - it's actually quite tightly plotted.

CONSISTENCY WITH PLAN: The chapter hits its planned beats well - second blood text arrives, effects kit tampered with, group chat explosion, Ellie's phone found. The chapter plan is followed faithfully.

PROSE QUALITY: Clean, functional prose that serves the voice well. No purple prose or forced metaphor. Occasionally the writing is a touch mechanical, but this suits the first-person YA register.

chapter_raw_judge_text.3:
[Analysis]

This chapter demonstrates considerable competence in executing the darkly comic British YA brief. The voice is consistent and authentic, capturing Penny's particular brand of anxious competence with genuine wit. The chapter plan called for Penny and Mira investigating, getting interrupted by real life (coursework, family dinner), discovering similar texts sent to other Frightmare staff, Jamie being weird, and ending with Penny finding actual blood in her effects kit. The chapter largely delivers on these beats, though the "actual blood in effects kit" ending is replaced by Mira's cryptic messages about something left behind, which arguably works better dramatically.

The character work is strong. Penny's voice is consistent with her profile - the perfectionist who deflects, the person who notices the corn syrup consistency before anything else, the one who climbs out windows rather than confronting parents. Jamie's characterization is particularly well-handled: the zombie bite wound reveal is both funny and slightly sinister, and his "practicing. Or preparing." ambiguity at the end is genuinely effective. Mira's seventeen-message sequence is an excellent comic set piece that also efficiently conveys character.

The dialogue is mostly sharp and naturalistic. "Helping solve a mystery doesn't count as its own reward, that's literally just the plot of Scooby-Doo" from the character profiles doesn't appear here, but the actual dialogue lands well. The suspects list in the notes app is a clever way to show Mira's character while advancing plot. The family dinner scene efficiently layers multiple tensions (drama school, the gap year lie, Jamie's knowledge) without feeling contrived.

The unknown number texts are the chapter's weakest element. "DINNER WITH THE FAMILY? SWEET. SHAME ABOUT ELLIE MISSING HERS." and "LEAVING WITHOUT TELLING YOUR PARENTS? TISK TISK" feel slightly too on-the-nose and melodramatic compared to the chapter's otherwise grounded tone. The "tisk tisk" misspelling is either a deliberate character detail or an error - it reads as the latter. These texts push toward thriller territory in a way that slightly undermines the darkly comic register.

The ending - "Practicing. Or preparing." - is effective without being overwrought. It earns its ambiguity. The chapter's pacing is good, moving efficiently between scenes without feeling rushed. The Business Economics coursework detail is exactly the kind of specific, grounding British teen detail the plan called for.

The "Every shadow looked wrong. Every puddle looked too red." passage is the chapter's most forced moment - it's trying for atmospheric effect but lands as slightly purple and clichéd. It's brief enough not to damage the chapter significantly.

The humor is character-driven rather than clever-narrative-tricks, as intended. The corn syrup subplot is genuinely funny and serves multiple purposes. The seventeen messages from Mira is the chapter's comedic highlight - it's perfectly calibrated.

Overall this is a solid, well-executed chapter that delivers on its plan, maintains consistent character voices, and balances comedy with genuine unease effectively. It's not exceptional - some thriller-text moments feel generic, and the atmospheric walking-home passage is weak - but it's competent, engaging work.

chapter_raw_judge_text.1:
[Analysis]

This opening chapter demonstrates considerable craft in establishing voice, tone, and character. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions.

VOICE AND CHARACTER: The first-person narration is genuinely strong. Penny's voice feels authentic to a 16-year-old British teen with specialist knowledge she's slightly too proud of. The opening gambit about fake blood recipes is an excellent character-establishing move - it tells us she's knowledgeable, slightly superior, but also genuinely passionate. The internal contradiction (pretending theatre is "just a hobby" while clearly living for it) comes through naturally rather than being stated. The detail about saving the unknown number "just in case" is a nice small character beat.

The year eight confrontation scene works well. Penny's competence is shown rather than told, and the "know-it-all" mutter from the departing student gives us social texture without belaboring it.

DIALOGUE: The dialogue is sparse but functional. The year eight exchange is brief and serves its purpose. The text messages from Mira are well-observed - the escalating panic, the "emotional range crisis" joke lands because it's specific. However, there's not much dialogue to truly evaluate.

PLOT AND MYSTERY: The chapter follows its plan well - establishing Frightmare Manor, the blood emoji texts, the power cut, and the mysterious drops. The final hook (the blood matching her secret recipe) is genuinely effective and raises the stakes cleverly. It connects the mystery to Penny's specific expertise in a way that feels organic rather than contrived.

TONE: The darkly comic British YA tone is well-calibrated. The humor doesn't undercut the genuine unease - the chapter earns its creepiness by first establishing Penny as someone who manufactures fake scares, making real ones more unsettling. The parenthetical "(ha)" after "dead" is a nice touch of self-aware humor that feels authentic to the character.

WEAKNESSES: The chapter occasionally tells rather than shows Penny's anxiety ("definitely not running out of the building, definitely not imagining more red drops" is a bit on-the-nose as a technique - the repeated denial is a well-worn device). The ending paragraph slightly over-explains the significance of the recipe detail, which the reader has already understood. "Award-worthy fake blood" is a small piece of self-congratulation that feels slightly forced.

The Mira texts, while charming, feel slightly convenient as a framing device - they appear exactly when needed to provide comic relief and then again at the end to restore normalcy.

BRITISH YA SPECIFICITY: The chapter does well here. Frightmare Manor, the sixth form context implied, the year eight/year nine distinctions, the Fleabag reference - these feel genuinely British rather than generically anglicized.

CONSISTENCY WITH PLAN: The chapter hits all its planned beats: late shift, year eights, group chat, blood emoji text, power cut. The ending with the drops matching her recipe adds a nice extra layer not explicitly in the plan but consistent with its spirit.

OVERALL: A confident, well-executed opening chapter that establishes character, tone, and mystery effectively. The voice is the strongest element. Some minor over-explanation and a slightly mechanical use of the Mira texts as comic relief bookends, but these are minor complaints against a genuinely engaging piece.

chapter_raw_judge_text.5:
[Analysis]

Chapter 5 of this darkly comic British YA fiction demonstrates considerable craft in several areas while showing some notable weaknesses worth examining carefully.

VOICE AND CHARACTER: Penny's voice is well-maintained throughout. The internal commentary feels authentic to the character profile - the special effects expertise bleeding into her observations ("Too thin. Too metallic"), the dry humor about horror movie clichés, the professional detachment masking genuine fear. Mira's presence through the headset is effective and consistent with her character profile - the true crime podcast obsession, the emergency preparedness, the "I have everyone's schedule. For investigative purposes" line lands well. Jamie's reveal as the prankster is consistent with his established character: the simultaneous desire to impress and annoy Penny, the fake blood theft, the theatrical flair.

PLOT AND STRUCTURE: The chapter follows the plan reasonably well - Halloween week begins, there's a real scare during performance, Penny spots something odd about blood patterns. The chapter plan mentioned "realizes connection to drama school rejection letters" which doesn't appear here, suggesting the model either deferred this or dropped it. The twist structure - fake blood leading to real blood, Jamie's prank leading to something genuinely threatening - is competent and creates genuine tension. The banter has genuine wit without feeling forced. The text messages as a narrative device work well for pacing, though they lean heavily on ALL CAPS which becomes slightly repetitive.

WEAKNESSES: The chapter relies heavily on horror movie cliché acknowledgment as a substitute for subverting those clichés. Penny literally says "Everything about this was becoming horrifically cliché" - this is a tell-don't-show problem. Acknowledging a cliché doesn't transcend it; the chapter still follows the exact beats it's commenting on. The "fake blood / real blood" twist is clever but the execution is somewhat mechanical - the reader is walked through Penny's deduction rather than experiencing it. The final reveal (someone with a real knife) is effective but the chapter has been building so many layers of fake-out that the genuine threat lands with slightly diminished impact. The "BOILING HOT" text message feels tonally off - it's almost playful in a way that doesn't quite fit the threatening register established earlier.

DARKLY COMIC BALANCE: The chapter mostly succeeds at balancing genuine menace with humor. The Mira headset exchanges provide levity without undercutting tension. The Jamie reveal is satisfying as a mid-chapter twist. However, the final beat pivots hard into genuine horror territory, which is appropriate for the chapter plan but leaves the "darkly comic" balance somewhat abandoned at the close.

PROSE QUALITY: Clean and functional. No purple prose or forced metaphors. The writing is appropriately spare for the genre and voice. Some sentences are slightly repetitive in structure (short declarative sentences stacked for effect), which works for pacing but occasionally feels mechanical.

COHERENCE: The chapter is coherent and well-structured. The logic of the investigation follows naturally. The headset cutting out is a convenient plot device but not egregiously so.

chapter_raw_judge_text.8:
[Analysis]

Chapter 8 serves as the denouement of the story, and it has genuine strengths alongside notable weaknesses. Let me work through these carefully.

STRENGTHS:

The chapter successfully captures Penny's voice - the dry, slightly superior tone of someone who knows their craft and is comfortable in their expertise. The detail about having memorized the recipe book years ago is a genuinely good character beat: it reveals something true about Penny (her perfectionism, her competence) while also providing a satisfying thematic resolution. The Jamie dynamic feels authentic - the sibling banter about "proper theatre people" lands naturally.

The structure of the mysterious texts shifting from threatening to directorial notes is a clever evolution that fits the story's darkly comic tone. The "EVERYONE'S A CRITIC" text is probably the chapter's best moment - genuinely funny and tonally right.

WEAKNESSES:

The chapter's most significant problem is its ending, which collapses into a series of increasingly strained "poetic" statements that don't earn their weight. "The curtain never really falls. It just waits for the next act to begin." This is the kind of theatrical-sounding profundity that sounds meaningful but is actually quite hollow. The repetition of "Even if it's just..." constructions is a classic amateurish move - using anaphora to create the impression of depth where there isn't much.

The mystery resolution is deeply unsatisfying. We never learn who "The Director" is. The chapter explicitly refuses to resolve this, framing the ambiguity as artistically intentional ("Would it make a better story if I did?"). But this reads less as sophisticated narrative restraint and more as the author avoiding the hard work of a satisfying resolution. The plan promised "Actual culprit not who anyone expected" in Chapter 7, and Chapter 8 was supposed to explain some mysteries while leaving others open - but this chapter leaves the central mystery entirely unresolved without sufficient justification.

The "Sophie incident" is referenced but never explained to the reader, which creates confusion rather than intrigue. We're told things are resolved without experiencing the resolution.

The dialogue is functional but thin. The Jamie exchanges are the best parts, but they're doing a lot of heavy lifting for a chapter that needs to land emotionally. Mira's appearance is reduced to a single text message, which wastes a well-developed character.

The forced poetry at the end is the chapter's biggest failure. "Even in fake blood. Even in theatre. Even in life." This is the kind of writing that sounds profound in a first draft and embarrassing on revision. The triple repetition of "Even" followed by increasingly abstract nouns is a technique that needs to be earned through the preceding narrative, and it isn't here.

The chapter follows the plan loosely - aftermath is dealt with, some mysteries are explained (vaguely), Penny makes a decision about drama school (implied rather than shown), and the final blood emoji appears. But the plan promised more resolution than is delivered.

The "Well-earned Lightness or Darkness" is mixed - the lightness of the ending feels somewhat earned by the character work throughout, but the mysterious director angle feels unearned because we don't know who it is.

The chapter is coherent within itself but relies heavily on events from previous chapters that we haven't read, making it feel somewhat opaque as a standalone piece.

[Analysis]

Chapter 4 of this darkly comic British YA mystery demonstrates considerable craft in several areas while showing some notable weaknesses worth examining carefully.

VOICE AND CHARACTER: Penny's voice is largely consistent with her established profile - the special effects expertise bleeding into her observations (noting the viscosity of the blood, identifying it as "wrong consistency"), the controlled panic, the protective instinct toward Jamie. The chapter captures her characteristic mix of competence and anxiety well. Mira's role as the pragmatic, true-crime-obsessed sidekick lands effectively, particularly her resigned "Fine. But I'm bringing my true crime emergency kit." Jamie's evasiveness and the hint of genuine fear beneath his usual annoying-younger-brother persona is handled with reasonable subtlety.

PLOT AND STRUCTURE: The chapter follows its plan closely - parents' evening backdrop, Jamie's suspicious behavior, another teen missing (Ellie Chen referenced), Penny getting information but not the full picture, Mira's involvement. The escalating text messages create genuine tension. The final "LITERALLY" pun on "dying" is a strong darkly comic beat that earns its place.

DIALOGUE: The dialogue is generally functional and occasionally sharp. "That's literally just the plot of Scooby-Doo" feels authentic to Jamie's character profile. However, some exchanges feel slightly mechanical - the Mira-Penny back-and-forth about whether to go to Frightmare reads as a checklist of expected beats (obviously a trap / obviously / so we're not going / I looked at her / she sighed). This is competent but not particularly surprising.

SHOW VS TELL: The chapter mostly shows rather than tells, using the text messages cleverly as a device to externalize threat. The blood fingerprint discovery is effectively dramatized. However, some emotional beats are stated rather than earned - "He went pale" is functional but not vivid.

PACING AND TENSION: The escalating text messages work well as a tension device, though by the fourth or fifth unknown-number text in the chapter, the pattern becomes slightly predictable. The chapter understands how to build dread through accumulation.

CREATIVITY: The "GOOD ARTISTS COPY / GREAT ARTISTS STEAL / BEST ARTISTS IMPROVE" message written in fake blood is genuinely clever - it connects to the drama/arts theme, creates menace, and has thematic resonance. The mystery of who's sending the texts remains genuinely intriguing. The "true crime emergency kit" is a nice character detail.

WEAKNESSES: The chapter leans heavily on the text-message-as-plot-device structure, which risks feeling gimmicky by this point in the story. Jamie's disappearance into the crowd is a slightly convenient narrative trick. The "I looked at her / She sighed" beat is a well-worn YA shorthand. Some of the unknown-number texts feel slightly too on-the-nose in their menace ("WANT ME TO DEAL WITH HIM?") - the villain feels a bit too omniscient and theatrical in a way that strains credibility even for a darkly comic mystery.

BRITISH YA AUTHENTICITY: The parents' evening setting is well-chosen and specific. The sixth form hall, Mr. Patterson, Business Studies - these feel grounded. The texting conventions feel authentic.

DARK COMEDY BALANCE: The chapter maintains the tonal balance reasonably well. The humor doesn't undercut the genuine threat, and the threat doesn't overwhelm the comic elements. The final "LITERALLY" is the chapter's best moment.

Overall this is a competent, engaging chapter that executes its plan faithfully with some genuinely strong moments, but it doesn't quite transcend its genre conventions or surprise the reader in ways that would mark it as exceptional.

[Analysis]

Chapter 6 "Stage Fright" is a tightly paced action sequence that largely succeeds at what it sets out to do: deliver a darkly comic thriller climax with Penny's special effects expertise at the center. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions.

VOICE AND CHARACTER: Penny's voice comes through reasonably well. The internal commentary ("What would a horror movie character do? Die, probably. What would a special effects expert do?") is genuinely funny and consistent with her established character profile. Her calm-in-crisis personality is demonstrated rather than stated, which is good. Jamie's reluctant-but-capable dynamic with Penny also lands, particularly his grin at the prospect of explosions. The sibling chemistry feels authentic. However, the chapter is so action-focused that there's little room for the deeper character nuance established in the profiles — Penny's anxieties about drama school, her complicated feelings about her parents, her secret crush on Leo — none of these surface meaningfully.

DIALOGUE: The dialogue is functional and occasionally sharp. "If we do it right" is a nice beat. The exchange about the prosthetic arm is good. However, some lines feel like they exist purely to advance plot mechanics rather than reveal character. The villain's "Come out, come out" and "Don't you want to know what happened to Ellie?" is a bit stock-horror-movie. The text messages as chapter punctuation (SURPRISE, GAME OVER, FINAL ACT BEGINNING / BREAK A LEG / LITERALLY) are clever in concept but the execution feels slightly forced — particularly "LITERALLY" as a standalone line, which tries too hard for comic effect.

PLOT AND COHERENCE: The chapter follows its plan reasonably well — investigation leads to drama department, Jamie reveals crucial information, Penny figures out part of the truth, everything goes wrong. The action sequence is coherent and the fake blood capsule solution is satisfying because it uses established character expertise. However, some elements are underdeveloped: the two masked figures are never differentiated, the connection to drama school rejection letters (promised in Chapter 5's plan) doesn't materialize here, and the red herrings involving the drama teacher are absent. The chapter plan promised "multiple reveals (some real, some red herrings)" but this chapter is almost entirely chase sequence with minimal revelation.

TONE: The darkly comic British YA tone is maintained. The humor is character-based rather than forced, which is the right approach. "Professionally insulting, really" about her zombie masks being stolen is a genuinely good line. The balance between genuine threat and absurdist comedy works more often than it doesn't.

WEAKNESSES: The chapter leans heavily on action-sequence shorthand — short sentences, fragmented thoughts, rapid cuts — which creates pace but sacrifices depth. The ending ("This is my stage") is a bit on-the-nose, telegraphing the theme rather than embodying it. The text messages as villain communication device, while established earlier in the story, starts to feel gimmicky by the third or fourth instance in this chapter. The chapter plan called for "Penny figures out part of truth" but she doesn't actually figure out much — she's mostly reacting. The promised connection to drama school rejection letters is entirely absent.

AMATEURISH ELEMENTS: The "LITERALLY" standalone line is the most amateurish moment — it's the kind of emphasis that reads as trying too hard. The villain's dialogue is generic. Some of the action beats are slightly repetitive (multiple instances of "both figures" doing things in parallel).

OVERALL: A competent, entertaining chapter that delivers on its core promise of action and character voice, but sacrifices depth and some planned plot elements for pace. It's enjoyable YA fiction without being exceptional.

chapter_raw_judge_text.7:
[Analysis]

Chapter 7 of this darkly comic British YA mystery is a fast-paced, action-driven climax chapter that largely succeeds at what it sets out to do, though with some notable weaknesses worth examining carefully.

Starting with the positives: the chapter has genuine energy and moves at a brisk pace appropriate for a climax. The dialogue is snappy and often genuinely funny, particularly the exchanges between Penny and Jamie about the year three nativity ("You were a convincing sheep!" / "You were not."), which feels authentically sibling-like and consistent with the character profiles. The humor lands more often than it doesn't, which is the primary goal of darkly comic YA fiction. The ending with the second masked figure escaping with the recipe book and the ominous sequel texts is a smart structural choice that honors the plan's note about leaving things unresolved.

The chapter plan called for "Halloween show chaos," "multiple reveals (some real, some red herrings)," "actual culprit not who anyone expected," "Penny's effects knowledge saves situation," and "some questions left unanswered." The chapter delivers on most of these, though the culprit (Sophie Miller, a jealous former makeup artist) is somewhat telegraphed by the clue-dropping in the confrontation scene. The second masked figure escaping is the genuinely surprising element.

Now for the criticisms:

The chapter is quite thin on emotional depth. For a climax chapter, there's surprisingly little interiority from Penny. The plan emphasized that "mystery elements serve character development rather than other way around," but here the mystery completely dominates. We get almost no access to Penny's internal state during what should be a high-stakes, emotionally resonant sequence. The character profiles describe her as "eerily calm in actual crises" but also someone with genuine anxieties about drama school and family — none of that surfaces here.

The reveal of Sophie Miller as the culprit is handled too quickly and too cleanly. Sophie's motivation (jealousy over losing a makeup job to a teenager) is stated rather than shown, and her confession is almost cartoonishly convenient. The "Ellie was just at her aunt's in Bristol" resolution deflates the mystery rather than satisfying it — it's meant to be darkly comic but lands closer to anticlimactic.

The dialogue, while often witty, sometimes tips into the territory of characters being too clever and self-aware in ways that strain credibility. "Dramatic timing. Also, traffic." is a good line. But the extended banter during what is supposedly a dangerous confrontation with knife-wielding figures occasionally breaks the tension in ways that feel more like the author winking at the reader than characters behaving authentically.

The quick-change sequence and the "I copied their mask exactly" moment is a fun theatrical trick, but it's not set up earlier in the chapter or (as far as we know) in previous chapters, making it feel slightly like a deus ex machina. The plan mentioned Penny's effects knowledge saving the situation, which this does, but the execution feels rushed.

Mira's arrival ("Also, traffic") and Leo's cricket bat entrance are fun but feel slightly too convenient. The chapter plan mentioned "everything goes wrong" in Chapter 6 leading into this, but Chapter 7 actually goes quite right for the protagonists until the final twist.

The prose itself is clean and functional — no purple prose, no forced metaphors. The opening lines about practical effects are genuinely good and thematically resonant. The writing is competent throughout without being particularly distinguished.

The characters are broadly consistent with their profiles: Penny is calm under pressure and uses her effects knowledge; Jamie is reluctant but helpful; Mira is chaotic and resourceful; Leo is present but peripheral. However, the nuance from the character profiles (Penny's anxieties about drama school, Mira's divorce situation, Jamie's complicated feelings about theatre) is entirely absent from this chapter.

The chapter is faithful to the writing prompt in that it maintains the darkly comic tone and the blood emoji mystery. It follows the chapter plan reasonably well, hitting most of the planned beats.

Overall: a competent, entertaining climax chapter that prioritizes plot mechanics and witty dialogue over emotional depth and character nuance. It's fun but somewhat shallow, and the resolution of the main mystery is too neat even as it opens a new thread.

final_raw_judge_texts:
[Analysis]

This is a competent, entertaining piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that largely succeeds at its genre aims. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions.

STRENGTHS:

The voice is the story's greatest asset. Penny's first-person narration is consistently sharp, self-aware, and genuinely funny in a British teenage way. The fake blood expertise as character foundation is clever and original - it gives her a distinctive lens through which everything is filtered, and the author commits to it throughout. Lines like "Professionally insulting, really" about the villain wearing her zombie mask, or the nativity sheep argument with Jamie, feel authentically teenage and British.

The structure is well-handled. The story builds momentum across chapters, and the pacing within individual chapters is generally good. The group chat sequences are particularly well-executed - they feel authentic to how teenagers actually communicate and serve narrative purposes without feeling forced.

The darkly comic tone is maintained consistently. The story knows what it is and doesn't overreach into genuine horror or undercut itself with excessive sentimentality. The balance between real threat and absurdist comedy is mostly well-calibrated.

Jamie as a character is genuinely charming - the sibling dynamic feels real, and his reveal as someone who was investigating independently rather than being complicit is satisfying.

WEAKNESSES:

The mystery resolution is significantly undercooked. Sophie Miller as the villain is telegraphed poorly - she appears briefly in Chapter 2 and then vanishes until the reveal. There's no meaningful foreshadowing of her motive (jealousy over the makeup artist job) that would make the reveal feel earned. The "lost the lead makeup artist job to a teenager" explanation comes out of nowhere and is delivered in a single line. This is a structural problem.

The "Act Two" sequel hook at the end - the mysterious Director who is apparently a separate, benevolent figure - is intriguing but also somewhat deflating. It undermines the resolution of the Sophie plot by suggesting the real mystery was never solved, and the final chapter's tone shifts into something more philosophical that doesn't quite earn its meditative quality. The "Director" figure is never explained, which is presented as mysterious but reads more as an unresolved plot thread.

The Ellie Chen subplot is handled lazily. She was simply paid to turn off her phone - this is a reasonable twist but it's delivered in a single throwaway line from Sophie. Given how much narrative weight Ellie's disappearance carries in the early chapters, this deflation feels cheap rather than darkly comic.

The dialogue in the confrontation scenes (Chapters 5-7) becomes somewhat generic thriller dialogue. "Come out, come out" and "Finally figured it out?" are stock villain lines that don't match the story's otherwise fresh voice.

The final chapter's philosophical musing ("the space between performance and reality") tips into unearned profundity. The story has been a fun, grounded thriller-comedy, and the ending reaches for thematic depth it hasn't quite built toward.

Mira is entertaining but essentially a plot device - the "true crime podcast obsessive" is a recognizable YA archetype without much depth beyond her function.

The "unknown Director" figure sending encouraging texts about drama school auditions is tonally jarring - it shifts from threatening to mentorly without explanation, and the protagonist's acceptance of this is presented as sophisticated but reads as slightly odd.

The action sequences in Chapters 5-7 are competent but rely heavily on convenient timing (the automated scream track, Mira's backup arriving perfectly). The story acknowledges this self-referentially, which partially defuses it, but doesn't fully earn the convenience.

OVERALL: This is solidly above-average YA genre fiction with a genuinely distinctive voice and some real wit. Its weaknesses are structural (underdeveloped villain, lazy subplot resolution, unearned philosophical ending) rather than fundamental. It's entertaining and readable throughout, but doesn't quite achieve the heights its premise and voice suggest it could.

[Analysis]

Chapter 2 of this darkly comic British YA fiction demonstrates considerable competence in voice, pacing, and tonal balance. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions.

VOICE AND CHARACTER: Penny's first-person narration is consistently well-rendered. The detail about Ellie borrowing liquid latex and returning it "mostly full" is an excellent character beat - it establishes Penny's relationship to Ellie through the lens of her craft without being heavy-handed. The internal logic of her character (perfectionist about effects kit alignment, the logbook always perfectly aligned) pays off effectively when these details become plot-relevant. Mira's true crime obsession is deployed with good comic timing, and the Ted Bundy/emoji exchange is genuinely funny without feeling forced. Jamie's blackmail via drama school applications is consistent with his profile and adds texture.

DIALOGUE: The group chat sequence is a genuine strength. It captures authentic teen communication patterns - the way information leaks despite attempts to contain it, the overlapping messages, Mira's "sorry it slipped out" - all ring true. The spoken dialogue is also solid, with the "fine/four times" exchange being a well-worn but effectively executed bit of comic business.

PLOT AND MYSTERY: The chapter escalates tension competently. The logbook entry ("NICE RECIPE. NEEDS MORE IRON THOUGH.") is a strong, creepy detail. The blood bottle discovery and the revelation that someone has been in the effects room builds genuine unease. The chapter ending with Ellie's phone found covered in blood is effective as a cliffhanger. However, the mystery elements are fairly conventional - the "someone was here before me" revelation follows a predictable horror/thriller template.

TONAL BALANCE: The darkly comic tone is well-maintained. The humor doesn't undercut the genuine menace, and the menace doesn't overwhelm the comedy. This is the chapter's strongest achievement.

WEAKNESSES: The chapter is somewhat formulaic in its mystery construction. The "everything felt off" beat followed by specific displaced objects is a well-worn thriller convention. The "More metallic. More like... No. No way. That's ridiculous." internal monologue is a bit clichéd - the ellipsis-and-denial structure is overused in YA fiction. The chapter follows its plan faithfully but doesn't significantly exceed it. Some of the group chat feels slightly convenient as an exposition delivery mechanism. The chapter doesn't quite achieve the "unresolved threads and messy emotions" the plan promised - it's actually quite tightly plotted.

CONSISTENCY WITH PLAN: The chapter hits its planned beats well - second blood text arrives, effects kit tampered with, group chat explosion, Ellie's phone found. The chapter plan is followed faithfully.

PROSE QUALITY: Clean, functional prose that serves the voice well. No purple prose or forced metaphor. Occasionally the writing is a touch mechanical, but this suits the first-person YA register.

[Analysis]

This chapter demonstrates considerable competence in executing the darkly comic British YA brief. The voice is consistent and authentic, capturing Penny's particular brand of anxious competence with genuine wit. The chapter plan called for Penny and Mira investigating, getting interrupted by real life (coursework, family dinner), discovering similar texts sent to other Frightmare staff, Jamie being weird, and ending with Penny finding actual blood in her effects kit. The chapter largely delivers on these beats, though the "actual blood in effects kit" ending is replaced by Mira's cryptic messages about something left behind, which arguably works better dramatically.

The character work is strong. Penny's voice is consistent with her profile - the perfectionist who deflects, the person who notices the corn syrup consistency before anything else, the one who climbs out windows rather than confronting parents. Jamie's characterization is particularly well-handled: the zombie bite wound reveal is both funny and slightly sinister, and his "practicing. Or preparing." ambiguity at the end is genuinely effective. Mira's seventeen-message sequence is an excellent comic set piece that also efficiently conveys character.

The dialogue is mostly sharp and naturalistic. "Helping solve a mystery doesn't count as its own reward, that's literally just the plot of Scooby-Doo" from the character profiles doesn't appear here, but the actual dialogue lands well. The suspects list in the notes app is a clever way to show Mira's character while advancing plot. The family dinner scene efficiently layers multiple tensions (drama school, the gap year lie, Jamie's knowledge) without feeling contrived.

The unknown number texts are the chapter's weakest element. "DINNER WITH THE FAMILY? SWEET. SHAME ABOUT ELLIE MISSING HERS." and "LEAVING WITHOUT TELLING YOUR PARENTS? TISK TISK" feel slightly too on-the-nose and melodramatic compared to the chapter's otherwise grounded tone. The "tisk tisk" misspelling is either a deliberate character detail or an error - it reads as the latter. These texts push toward thriller territory in a way that slightly undermines the darkly comic register.

The ending - "Practicing. Or preparing." - is effective without being overwrought. It earns its ambiguity. The chapter's pacing is good, moving efficiently between scenes without feeling rushed. The Business Economics coursework detail is exactly the kind of specific, grounding British teen detail the plan called for.

The "Every shadow looked wrong. Every puddle looked too red." passage is the chapter's most forced moment - it's trying for atmospheric effect but lands as slightly purple and clichéd. It's brief enough not to damage the chapter significantly.

The humor is character-driven rather than clever-narrative-tricks, as intended. The corn syrup subplot is genuinely funny and serves multiple purposes. The seventeen messages from Mira is the chapter's comedic highlight - it's perfectly calibrated.

Overall this is a solid, well-executed chapter that delivers on its plan, maintains consistent character voices, and balances comedy with genuine unease effectively. It's not exceptional - some thriller-text moments feel generic, and the atmospheric walking-home passage is weak - but it's competent, engaging work.
